Email #1, from Cameron (scam-baiter):

Dear Assistant Commissioner God-Win Johnson,

I hope this message finds you well. It was with great enthusiasm that I received your prompt and encouraging response. Learning of Pastor Dorcas Paul’s unwavering faith amidst her trials has truly been an inspiration to me. Her determination to turn her plight into a blessing for others in need speaks volumes of her character, and it reinforces my commitment to ensure her wishes are fulfilled with the respect and dedication they deserve.

I am heartened to hear of the actions taken by your office in coordination with the Wells Fargo Bank, Chicago Branch, and am eagerly anticipating contact from Mr. James Williams or his team. It is my honor to be a part of this noble endeavor, and I promise to handle the responsibility with the integrity and seriousness it requires.

Regarding the request for my identification details, I understand the necessity for such information to process the allocation of the fund. However, in today’s digital age, it is paramount to exercise caution when sharing sensitive personal information. I trust you can appreciate my concerns, given the unfortunate prevalence of online fraud and identity theft.

Therefore, in the spirit of due diligence, would it be possible to firstly have a brief video call or meeting with Mr. James Williams or a representative from the Wells Fargo Bank? This would not only serve to verify the authenticity of the charitable outreach but also provide an avenue to discuss the process comprehensively.

Furthermore, could you please provide me with official documentation or a link to the Wells Fargo Bank’s specific project or philanthropic division handling Pastor Dorcas Paul’s funds? This will allow me to review the legal and procedural framework in advance and prepare any necessary documentation from my end.

I hope you understand that these requests are made in good faith, aiming to protect all parties involved and ensuring a smooth and transparent process. Once verification is complete, I will be more than willing to comply with all necessary steps to move forward.

I honor the trust Pastor Dorcas Paul and you have in me and look forward to proceeding with the promise of impacting lives positively in her name. Thank you once again for allowing me to be part of this significant mission. May our efforts bring solace to Pastor Dorcas and honor her legacy in a manner befitting her generous spirit.

God bless,

Cameron McCaffrey

However, Pastor Dorcas Paul, has made me to understand today that She has asked his family attorney to-send to bank THE $20.2 MILLION WILL DOCUMENT) and copy of it forwarded to you.

Please,do keep me updated upon your hearing from the Wells Fargo Bank of USA.

You are to send the below:

2----------------- Your bank account

ASSISTANT COMMISSIONER OF POLICE
GOD-WIN JOHNSON
Director of operation France/Peru operational unit.

Email #3, from Cameron (scam-baiter):

Dear Assistant Commissioner God-Win Johnson,

I trust this message finds you in good spirits. I am writing to express my sincere gratitude for your swift action and unwavering dedication towards facilitating the transfer of the $20.2 million fund as bestowed by Pastor Mrs. Dorcas Paul. It is truly heartening to see such commitment to her cause, and it reinforces my faith in the integrity of this process.

I must admit, the expectation of hearing from the Wells Fargo Bank has filled me with an immense sense of responsibility and anticipation. I am very much looking forward to contributing to Pastor Dorcas's legacy and ensuring her charitable desires are met with the respect and diligence they deserve.

Regarding the information requested in your previous email, I understand the necessity of providing my bank account details to facilitate the fund transfer. However, as I mentioned earlier, I hold a strong commitment to ensuring all precautions are taken to secure the safety of all parties involved in this noble transaction.

Therefore, before proceeding with the provision of my banking information, I respectfully request to establish a more direct line of communication with the Wells Fargo Bank's representative handling Pastor Dorcas's fund. A brief video call or an in-person meeting, if geographically feasible, would serve as an ideal platform to discuss the necessary steps and to personally verify the authenticity of this endeavor. I believe this will not only fortify our mutual trust but also streamline the process, ensuring a seamless execution of Pastor Dorcas's wishes.

Further, to facilitate a smooth and transparent process, I would be grateful if you could share any official documentation or a direct link to the specific philanthropic project within the Wells Fargo Bank that outlines their protocol for handling such charitable funds. This documentation would be valuable for my personal record and further understanding of the procedural intricacies involved.

I want to reiterate my dedication to this cause and assure you that my requests for additional verifications stem from a place of due diligence and responsibility towards Pastor Dorcas's legacy. Once these preliminary verifications are satisfactorily completed, I will be more than prepared to proceed with providing the necessary information and moving forward with our shared mission.

I eagerly await your understanding and cooperation in this matter, and please do keep me informed of any developments from your end or the Wells Fargo Bank. Together, I am confident we can honor Pastor Dorcas Paul's memory in a manner that truly reflects her generous and philanthropic spirit.

God bless, and looking forward to your prompt response.

Warm regards,

Cameron McCaffrey

Cameron, hello, my dear! I am glad to see your letter!
Please don't be upset if I don't answer all your thoughts and questions today.
Thank you for showing your passion and caring for me and not hiding your emotions. I really appreciate that about you.
I have some good news today.
I talked to my boss and she said that I can count on my vacation in about 10 days.
I have to finish my work, and then I can go on vacation.
I am looking forward to the vacation so that we can make an appointment and not write these letters endlessly.
Do you want us to meet? I'm sure that's what you want! :)
I have never traveled to other countries except Belarus (Minsk), Georgia. Egypt (Hurghada and Sharm el Sheikh),
Turkey (Antalya, Alanya), United Arab Emirates (Dubai, Sharjah). The last time I left Russia, I was in Georgia, and that was in 2020.
At the moment, I have been working without a vacation for 15 months.

This year, at the beginning of the school year, I renovated my personal apartment, but I still have some savings left.
I travel a lot in Russia, but this year I definitely want to travel outside of Russia!
I have visited Sochi, St. Petersburg, Irkutsk (on Lake Baikal), Kazan, and more recently Yekaterinburg and many other cities.
Novosibirsk, Nizhny Novgorod, Rostov-on-Don, I can list for a long time.
I believe that everyone should first study the history of their homeland before exploring the world.
But I want to see new countries, the world! If you want, I can fly to you.
It's been a long time since I've felt anything like what I feel in my heart right now. I have an interest in you...
I look forward to receiving your new emails every day.

Until now, I had no idea that I could experience sublime feelings and receive some pleasant emotions from communicating with a person from another country through virtual communication.
I'm being honest with you and telling you how I feel, I want to share all my thoughts with you.
From the very beginning of our conversation, I told you that you should be sincere, and now I trust you.
I feel that you have become a dear person to me, as if I have known you for a very long time. I feel like we have soul mates.
Repeat your question if I forgot to answer your questions.
Sometimes I don't have time to reply, but you can be sure that I always read your emails. Our friendship is very pleasant.
What do you want in the future, and do you want me to be a part of your future?
I have a passport that allows me to travel the world.
I don't have a visa to fly to you, but I think it's not that difficult to get a visa.
I really want to see your country and your way of life.
Maybe very soon we will be able to ride horses together or go to a concert, listen to live music, play bowling or swim!
It all depends on our desire!
I am sending an email with my photos. You can see that I smile when I think of you.I've never smiled in a photo before.

I don't understand what happened to me. My life and my inner world have changed for me.
I don't want to scare you, but I hope you understand... I appreciate your company.
I am glad to see your letter. It always makes me smile! :-)
Tomorrow I will bake delicious sweet muffins with chocolate and cocoa filling. It's a pity you don't try them.
Please forgive me, I have a very difficult day at work today and I can't write much. I just wanted to please you with my news.
I'm blowing kisses to you!
With great love, Svetlana.

Email Response from Cameron:

Dear Svetlana,

I am thrilled every time I receive a message from you; it feels like a ray of sunshine in the middle of a mundane day. Your words always carry such warmth and kindness; they truly brighten my world. I want you to know that your letters are a highlight of my day, too.

It's wonderful to hear about your upcoming vacation and the possibility of meeting in person! That thought alone fills me with excitement and a sense of anticipation that's hard to describe. Your travels sound amazing, and I admire your passion for exploring new places, both within Russia and abroad. It shows a beautiful curiosity and zest for life.

The idea of you visiting me is incredibly appealing. There are so many places I’d love to show you in my country, and experiences I'd be thrilled to share with you. Whether it's enjoying live music, exploring the outdoors, or simply spending time together, I believe it would be a memorable time for both of us.

Regarding your feelings and the connection we've been nurturing, I must say, the sentiment is mutual. I deeply value our growing bond and the sincerity of our communication. It’s rare to find someone with whom conversations flow so effortlessly, and even rarer when that person makes your heart skip a beat with just a few written words.

About the visa and travel arrangements, you're right; with the right information and approach, obtaining a visa can be a straightforward process. However, I want to ensure everything goes smoothly and safely. In the spirit of taking care of each other, I would like to help in any way I can with your travel plans. Maybe there are specific procedures or requirements for the visa application that I could assist you with from my end.

On a lighter note, the way you describe your baking makes me wish I could teleport myself to your kitchen to try those sweet muffins! It truly is a pity that distance separates us, but perhaps, it’s just a matter of time before we can enjoy such simple pleasures together.

As for your question about the future, knowing you want me to be part of it fills me with joy and hope. The future is unknowable, but the thought of exploring it with you makes me look forward to whatever comes our way.

Please don't worry if work keeps you busy; I completely understand the demands of professional life. Just knowing you take the time to share your thoughts and feelings with me is more than I could ask for. You have nothing to apologize for. In fact, your dedication to your work is another aspect of you that I admire.

Looking forward to hearing from you soon. Until then, your letters and the thought of your smile will keep me company.

With thoughts of you,

Cameron

Format the following passage properly, for storytelling purposes.

As a writer, I have learned the importance of opening my mind to inspiration, in the many forms it takes. When I go outside for a walk, it is possible for me to be inspired by something as inconsequential as a hunk of stray metal, a discarded chip bag, a piece of cardboard, or a marker that someone accidentally left on the ground. In the past week alone, since I started going outdoors and getting more exercise, I've had the pleasure of finding various items that people have for whatever reason thrown away, my favorite (and honestly, only truly notable) finds thus far being a perfectly good backpack without any fabric rips or broken/stuck zippers, two laundry baskets (one ebony, another ivory) and a giant mirror with the Nike "Jumpman" logo in the upper-left-hand corner. As others of my generation will remember, the likeness immortalized in that silhouette is none other than the legendary Michael Jordan, and it is almost surreal to think that over twenty years have come and gone since he last set foot on an NBA court as a player. One of my earliest memories of watching sports on television was seeing Jordan and the Bulls defeat the Utah Jazz in the NBA Finals two years in a row. I also recall the dominance of another team, in another sport, during the same timeframe: the Green Bay Packers, a brilliant squadron commandeered by the great Mike Holmgren and the cannon arm of Brett Favre, who like the Bulls contended for world championships in consecutive years but failed to win titles back-to-back. I remember hearing the roar of the crowd, and seeing the excitement in John Elway's eyes, as his Broncos upset the Packers in Super Bowl XXXII, but not until I was older did I understand the significance of what they had pulled off. The Broncos had come badly short in four prior attempts to win a world championship, each one ending in a lopsided blowout (with their most recent defeat being a 55-10 shellacking against the 49ers), and the AFC had not beaten the NFC in the big game for more than a decade. As such, they were installed as eleven-point underdogs, which is to say they were given almost no chance. Living in America my whole life, I have begun to understand the significance it holds for Americans in particular, although I also aknowledge the shared global consciousness surrounding the concept of an underestimated 'black sheep' who rises against all odds and manages to achieve more than anyone had ever believed they would. It is seldom fundamentally distillable to the essence of victory versus defeat, and just as politics cannot be distilled to an essence of Republicans versus Democrats--if I may be bold enough to say so, there is not a dime's worth of difference between the parties--I have found many victories in life have come in what the majority would classify as defeats. One of my good friends who I keep in touch with, who is almost thirty years my senior, briefly spoke to me this morning. He told me to be careful with all decisions, as my parents' passing is still fresh. He said that in this life, situations will come our way, and one transgression (be it we who are guilty, or a person close to us) can start a chain reaction of events that may ultimately land us on the street. He told of a family who had taken him in when he was homeless, who apparently robbed him of his possessions while he was out of town for a few days and rendered him homeless again. Having experienced firsthand how treacherous certain individuals can be, I am convinced of the validity of his story, and there are moments where I contemplate the precarious mixture of ingredients in the soup that is my life. There are more cooks in the kitchen than there had been before, and I can only hope that their intentions are not impure, that none have intentions of incorporating arsenic into the mixture of ingredients. Consulting with Evelyn, I know I am going to get an honest response every time, and the challenge I face now when we are in conversation with one another is to make sure I'm listening to what she tells me, and to treat the feedback I receive not as condemnations of my character but as constructive feedback for improvement. Be it related to life choices (e.g. remembering that sometimes it's totally fine to let a call go to voicemail and that we don't have to answer the phone every time it rings) or even my own literary techniques (e.g. being aware of my deviation from particular topics and tendency to engage in tangential discussion), I realize that if she did not care she would not bother to say anything. I do not agree with everything say or do, but appreciate those who are making an effort to help me. I know they will not be around forever, and in fact one of them told me about the concept of the baton, how they can hand it to you but only you can make the choice to run with it, hand it off to someone else or abandon it and do something else. I feel an obligation to take the baton, knowing I will sometimes drop it but also knowing that what matters is not how many times you drop the baton, it is whether you are still holding onto it when you reach the finish line. Though it is a quality that can manifest itself in allistic individuals as well, I feel my autism amplifies the fascination I hold with certain aspects of this existence. To exaggerate somewhat, the aforementioned discarded chip bag will for most be nothing more than garbage, to be thrown away presuming it is outside a suitable receptacle and subsequently forgotten about. For me, that same chip bag invites different thoughts. It may compel me to visit my local supermarket and buy a fresh bag for myself. I may find myself enamored with the artwork on the bag. "Why did they choose these colors?" "That font looks nice; is it custom, commercial, or free?" In the most extreme cases, I may even write to the manufacturer of the chips to see if they can get me in touch with the artist who was originally responsible for the bag design. I tend to fall into the latter group, though I have yet to reach out to any graphic designers. Actually, as I've alluded to in earlier pieces, the main recipients of my affection have been composers and voice actors. I do not know what it is that specifically draws me to such individuals but the best presumption I've been able to make, in the more than twenty years that have come and gone since ten-year-old me wrote to Hynden Walch--pouring out a cascade of spontaneous, only loosely conscientious thoughts, with no rhyme or reason--is that it all goes back to my innate passion for music, one that has been nurtured since I had just barely graduated from being cosmic dust to residing in the warmth of my mother's womb. Even in conversations where individuals may demonstrate no blatant intent of communicating musicality, I am nonetheless able to discern musical elements within what they are articulating at a given time. The beauty of a sound reverberates, not only within my ears but also my spirit. This, for me, is where it becomes 'extreme'. I was the kind of kid who somehow was not content to change the channel right away, rewind the tape, or turn off the television set once a show was over. I stuck around for the credits and in many ways, those credits could be equally as interesting as the programs themselves. There was a distinct sort of satisfaction I felt when I could find out who did what, and even at a young age a desire to express a gratitude to those who made it possible, though it was not until I became older that the significance of what these artists did began to dawn on me with a newfound resonance. Twenty years after writing to Hynden Walch, I wrote to her again a few months back but have yet to receive a response as of the time of writing; revisiting the little piece that remains of what I had written, it is honestly not hard to see why, but damned if it didn't feel good to return to the forest of old memories. It is for this same reason that I felt compelled to invest in a couple more stamp books and some envelopes. Unfortunately, my favorite brand and style of bubble mailers were not available at my local Dollar Tree (more like $1.25 + Tax Tree these days), and the last few I had were used to send unreciprocated Christmas messages to a few voice actors in Japan, a twofold gamble since many agencies in Japan do not permit their talents to respond to fan mail and I was reaching out to veterans whose performances I had grown up with, as opposed to younger, fresher faces who may be far more inclined to respond in a timely manner. I don't regret it either way, because the feeling of putting pen to paper and writing from your heart is the apex of satisfaction. Wanting to know this feeling again, I am tempted to write a letter or two, since I do not resume my volunteering at the achievement center for another week. I have a clear idea of who I want to get in touch with, but at the same time I feel hesitation. One of these individuals is someone I first wrote to more than two decades ago, and had recently contacted more than once by email. Though it is partially informed by my parents and their religious beliefs I've long held a view that when we reach out to someone and do not hear back, oftentimes that is an external force trying to activate a defense mechanism, so that we may be protected from that which is not meant for us, from those whose destinies are not meant to collide with ours. At the same time, it is difficult to tell when this is and is not true, and I've come to find that the best way of dealing with the idle thoughts that come when we fall into the pit of contemplation is to just stay busy with other interests that bring us joy. We are all busy with something, in one form or another, and for all we know the folks we may be excited to hear back from are hard at work, involved with projects that will bring a little more enrichment to this world. I know I was never the dumbest, but I also know I will never be the smartest, but I am smart enough to have learned a valuable lesson: the love we give is frequently even more powerful than the love we receive. We should be thankful for every bit of love that we get, but at the same time we should never become weary in the act of giving love if ever we start to feel that it is anything less than worthwhile. From a homeless man on the corner to some of the most intelligent thinkers in world history, including Nobel Prize winners and others who made significant strides in science, literature, philosophy, etc. we've all felt and expressed unrequited love at one time or another. In the end, I believe it is best to hold onto a loving spirit. Our thoughts, once released into the ether, are unpredictable merchants of fate. If it is meant to be, we will hear back from those with whom we have shared those thoughts. If the ship of inevitability was destined to navigate different seas, we have still won a great victory just by making the effort to reach out. The liberation and satisfaction that comes with exercising the mind is cathartic, and it can count as the completion of a goal we have set for ourselves. Too often, people say they'll do something and never follow through. In my travels I have come across precious few people who have made the effort to reach out to notable individuals in different fields. Maybe it's a matter of being 'busy', or not wanting to be a nuisance. I have such an admiration for the people with enough restraint to forgo reaching out to the ones who help the wheels of creativity to turn worldwide. Even at the risk of alienating those I admire, I refuse to apply such restraints to myself. Knowing many who inspire me may see me as a complete jackass, I continue to reach out, knowing the odds are always against me, knowing the reward is always sweeter when you're willing to take the risk, knowing the misses sculpt our growth just as much as the hits.
